rationale: The citing paper uses Yang, Toubia, and De Jong (2018) for a specific empirical claim: higher realization probability in conjoint tasks changes processing and outcomes, including greater price sensitivity and lower novelty seeking. Ellickson et al. use this evidence to motivate and interpret their own context-bias distortions when linking stated and revealed preference data, especially around price sensitivity. The citation also appears in their conclusion as a concrete direction for improving conjoint validity via incentive design, indicating a meaningful framing role rather than a passing mention.
relation_description: Ellickson et al. rely on Yang et al. (2018) as prior empirical support that incentive alignment and realization probability can distort conjoint preferences. This prior finding underpins their contextual-bias framing and supports their use of distribution-shifter adjustments when fusing conjoint and panel purchase data.
